term: hit the nail on the head
example:
You hit the nail on the head.
She hit the nail on the head.
That comment hit the nail on the head.

term: the ball is in your court
meaning: it's your decision or turn to act now
example:
I've done my part; the ball is in your court.
Now the ball is in your court.
The ball is in their court.

Stop beating around the bush.
Don't beat around the bush.
He kept beating around the bush.

He got cold feet before the wedding.
Don't get cold feet now.
She got cold feet at the last minute.

meaning: a small visible part of a much bigger problem
example:
These complaints are just the tip of the iceberg.
That's only the tip of the iceberg.
The cost is the tip of the iceberg.

If you wait, you'll miss the boat.
We missed the boat on that deal.
Don't miss the boat.

She's really on the ball.
You need to be on the ball.
He's always on the ball.

We don't always see eye to eye.
They see eye to eye on most things.
We finally saw eye to eye.

meaning: the advantages of two different things
example:
Working from home is the best of both worlds.
You get the best of both worlds.
It offers the best of both worlds.

Feel free to speak your mind.
She always speaks her mind.
Speak your mind honestly.

meaning: act too soon, before the right time
example:
Don't jump the gun.
He jumped the gun and announced it early.
We jumped the gun a bit.

term: by the skin of your teeth
example:
We caught the train by the skin of our teeth.
He passed by the skin of his teeth.
They won by the skin of their teeth.

meaning: a situation that is unclear or uncertain
example:
The law here is a grey area.
It's a bit of a grey area.
This falls into a grey area.

Don't sit on the fence — decide.
He's sitting on the fence.
Stop sitting on the fence.

She always goes the extra mile.
They go the extra mile for customers.
Going the extra mile pays off.

meaning: the most important fact or result
example:
The bottom line is cost.
What's the bottom line?
The bottom line is we need more time.

He's in hot water with the boss.
They landed in hot water.
You'll be in hot water for that.

meaning: use influence or connections to get something
example:
He pulled strings to get the job.
She knows how to pull strings.
They pulled a few strings.

The plan failed, so we're back to square one.
We're back to square one.
That puts us back to square one.

term: off the top of my head
example:
Off the top of my head, I'd say twenty.
I can't recall off the top of my head.
Off the top of my head, there are three.

meaning: an event that makes you realise you must change
example:
The illness was a wake-up call.
It was a real wake-up call.
The results were a wake-up call.

That idea gives me food for thought.
Here's some food for thought.
The talk was real food for thought.

Keep an eye on the kids.
Can you keep an eye on my bag?
Keep an eye on the time.

meaning: begin to understand or deal with something
example:
I'm getting to grips with the software.
We need to get to grips with the problem.
She got to grips with the job quickly.

They're going through a rough patch.
We hit a rough patch.
It was just a rough patch.

meaning: an extra good thing that makes something even better
example:
Winning was the icing on the cake.
The bonus was the icing on the cake.
That's just the icing on the cake.

term: give the benefit of the doubt
meaning: choose to trust someone despite some doubt
example:
I'll give him the benefit of the doubt.
Give her the benefit of the doubt.
They gave us the benefit of the doubt.

Starting a business is a leap of faith.
It took a leap of faith.
Sometimes you need a leap of faith.

Delays are par for the course here.
That's par for the course.
Long hours are par for the course.

term: cross that bridge when we come to it
meaning: deal with a problem when it happens, not before
example:
We'll cross that bridge when we come to it.
Let's cross that bridge when we come to it.
I'll cross that bridge when I come to it.
